explanation_md: `addTwo(3, 4)` instantiates `addTwo<int>` and returns `3 + 4 = 7`. `addTwo(1.5, 2.5)` instantiates `addTwo<double>` and returns `1.5 + 2.5 = 4.0`. Since `4.0` has no fractional part, `std::cout` prints `4`.

prompt_md: Inside a `template <typename T>` class, a member `operator==` needs to compare two instances of the same class type. Which declaration is correct?

options.a: `template <typename T> bool operator==(const MyClass<T>& rhs) const;`
options.c: `template <typename U> bool operator==(const MyClass<U>& rhs) const;`
options.d: `static bool operator==(const MyClass& a, const MyClass& b);`

explanation_md: Inside the template class body, `MyClass` already refers to `MyClass<T>` — the class's own type. A plain `bool operator==(const MyClass& rhs) const` is the correct member signature. Option A would shadow the outer `T`; option C compares different template instantiations; option D is a static function (not the same as a member operator).

prompt_md: When does the compiler report an error for a template function that uses `operator<` on type `T`?

options.a: When the template is first defined, even before it is called.
options.b: Only when the template is instantiated for a specific type that does not define `operator<`.
options.c: The compiler never reports this error — it silently uses integer comparison instead.
options.d: The linker reports the error, not the compiler.

explanation_md: Template bodies are not fully type-checked at the point of definition. The error surfaces only when the template is *instantiated* — when the compiler tries to generate code for a specific `T` that lacks `operator<`. This is why template errors sometimes appear far from the template's definition.

options.a: `rhs` should be passed by value, not by const reference.
options.b: Line X re-declares `T`, shadowing the class-level `T` — the inner `template <typename T>` is redundant and conflicts.
options.d: `operator==` must be a free function, not a member.

explanation_md: Inside a template class, member functions already have access to the class's template parameter `T`. Adding `template <typename T>` on the member function introduces a second `T` that shadows or conflicts with the outer one. Remove line X entirely — `bool operator==(const Pair& rhs) const` works correctly.
